howItWorks.intro: NEC Chapter 9 limits how much of a conduit's internal area can be filled with wires to prevent overheating and allow easy pulling.
rules: 1 wire: 53% fill allowed. 2 wires: 31% fill. 3 or more wires: 40% fill. These percentages are from NEC Chapter 9 Table 1
Wire areas come from NEC Chapter 9 Table 5 for THHN/THWN insulated conductors, which include the insulation thickness
Conduit internal areas vary by type — EMT, PVC Schedule 40, PVC Schedule 80, and rigid metal each have different inside diameters
howItWorks.outro: Overfilling conduit makes wire pulls difficult and traps heat, which reduces ampacity. Always check fill before starting a conduit run to avoid costly rework.

faq.question: What is the NEC 40% conduit fill rule?
faq.answer: When three or more conductors are in a conduit, NEC Chapter 9 Table 1 limits total wire area to 40% of the conduit's internal area. For one conductor it's 53%, and for two conductors it's 31%. These limits ensure wires can dissipate heat and be pulled without damage.

faq.question: How many 12 AWG THHN wires fit in 3/4-inch EMT?
faq.answer: Up to 16 conductors of 12 AWG THHN can fit in 3/4-inch EMT conduit while staying within the 40% fill limit. Each 12 AWG THHN wire has an area of 0.0133 sq inches, and 3/4-inch EMT has 0.213 sq inches of usable fill area.

faq.question: Does the ground wire count toward conduit fill?
faq.answer: Yes, equipment grounding conductors count toward conduit fill calculations. Every conductor inside the conduit — hot, neutral, and ground — takes up space and must be included in the fill calculation.

faq.question: What is the difference between EMT and rigid conduit for fill?
faq.answer: EMT (thin-wall) has a larger internal diameter than rigid metal conduit (RMC) of the same trade size, so it allows more wires. For example, 3/4-inch EMT has 0.533 sq in total area while 3/4-inch RMC has 0.549 sq in. Always use the correct table for your conduit type.

faq.question: Can I mix wire sizes in one conduit?
faq.answer: Yes, you can mix wire sizes. Calculate the total area of all conductors and compare it to the conduit's allowed fill area. This calculator uses a single wire size, but for mixed sizes, add up individual wire areas from NEC Chapter 9 Table 5.

faq.question: What happens if I exceed NEC conduit fill limits?
faq.answer: Overfilled conduit causes heat buildup that reduces wire ampacity, makes pulling difficult (risking insulation damage), and violates NEC code. An inspector will require you to either upsize the conduit or split wires into multiple conduits.
